data.title: Conrad Industries: Marine Vessel Builder Gaining Traction Through Its Growing Backlog
data.description:
Intro

Conrad Industries, Inc. (OTCPK:CNRD) operates in the shipbuilding industry in the US, with the likes of steel-based harbor tugs & specialty barges being some of the popular finished products that the company delivers. The company makes vessels both for the private & public sectors in the US. Conrad also repairs & converts vessels, as we will learn from its revenue numbers in its first quarter earnings report this year. The Louisiana-based outfit has now 75 years of shipbuilding experience under its belt and currently trades with a market cap of approximately $116 million. The company has been built on values such as solid craftsmanship, top-notch service to customers, and above all, integrity.

As we see from the technicals, shares have rallied more than $4 a share or 24% since the release of the company's first quarter earnings report on the 15th of May this year. This has resulted in shares fastly approaching multi-year overhead resistance (Particularly Conrad's 2007 & 2022 highs). Being able to break through this resistance level is essentially where the opportunity lies in this stock. The reason being is that there is very little resistance above the $17 to $18 level if indeed shares manage to break through.

Suffice it to say, given the violent rally we have seen in Conrad over the past 5 weeks, there is definitely opportunity in this play if trading conditions persist for the remainder of fiscal 2025.

Q1 Earnings Report

Total revenues in the first quarter amounted to $80.5 million, generating a net profit of $3.9 million for the quarter, easily beating the $1.5 million comparable in the same period of 12 months prior. This means trailing GAAP income amounts to $13.58 million, resulting in a trailing P/E ratio of 8.56. Operating income over the same 12-month period amounts to $15.03 million, resulting in a trailing price to EBIT ratio of 7.73.

This is an excellent start, given the inverse of the company's trailing P/E ratio (Trailing earnings yield of 11.68%) immediately informs us of what shareholders would earn if management decided to pay out all of its GAAP profits in the form of a dividend.

Revenue Growth To The Fore

The key to Conrad's boost in profitability in Q1 was down to its revenue growth, which spiked by $14.2 million or 17.6% in the first quarter. Due to high fixed costs in this industry (gross margin came in at 8.73% in Q1), Conrad has to be in a position to be able to turn over its capital at speed in order to boost its return on capital numbers. it did this in the first quarter of this year.

Essentially, investing in Conrad is a play on whether one believes forward-looking revenue growth rates can surpass rising costs, which are seen across the board. Not only has management had to deal with rising steel prices, but tariffs have placed an extra layer of uncertainty concerning Conrad's forward-looking build costs. Furthermore, given how inflation has been impacting wages in recent times, management has also had to increase pay to employees to remain competitive in a fast-changing labour market.

Suffice it to say, rising costs must be offset by economies of scale both from a new-build standpoint as well as in its repairs segment.

We state this because any slowdown in revenue growth will have negative consequences right down the income statement, affecting net profit margin as a result. From a balance sheet standpoint, we see that Conrad's trailing return on equity comes in at 15.93%, easily outperforming the company's cost of equity (demonstrating value is being created). Furthermore, the level of cash on the balance sheet ($35.89 million) and minimal debt demonstrate the strong efficiency of the company's 5 ship-building yards. These trends are encouraging, especially if accounts receivable can continue to be reined in at a faster clip than revenue growth over time.

Enduring Competitive Advantages Leading To Growing Backlog

Conrad's backlog now comes in at a very strong $292 million, highlighting competitive advantages Conrad has at its disposal, as we learn below from comments on the Q1 earnings report for fiscal 2025.

Although we face substantial uncertainties in our markets, we believe we enjoy many competitive advantages, including our shipyard capacity, our investments in improving our shipyards’ capabilities and efficiencies, our experienced management and engineering teams, and our many devoted employees. We also believe that we benefit from a reputation for producing quality products and for workplace safety, as evidenced by being awarded the 2023 and 2024 American Equity Underwriters, Inc. (AEU) Safety Award and both the 2023 and 2024 Shipbuilders Council of America (SCA) “Excellence in Safety” awards and the 2023 “Improvement in Safety” award.

Forward-Looking Risks

Being a micro-cap play brings its own set of risks, which we outline below. Firstly, the company trades with below-average trading volume, which means aggressive selling could easily push the stock below key support levels. Micro-cap plays accordingly have to be actively managed to ensure one's position is always aligned with the prevailing trend.

Although Conrad has been able to attain multiple competitive advantages in recent decades, high fixed-cost businesses are often at the mercy of economic conditions, adversely affecting commodity prices as a result. A rising backlog may demonstrate growing demand, but the macro-picture invariably tends to decide how fast these ships can be delivered.

Potential slower delivery speeds affect how fast management collects its debts, which is a crucial aspect of being able to boost the company's return on capital over time. Therefore, while we acknowledge Conrad's potential, the company's low profit margins demand that revenue growth must remain strong, so that consistent shareholder value can be created over time.

Conclusion

To sum up, we are stamping a 'Hold' rating on Conrad Industries for the moment, although we may upgrade the stock to a 'Buy ' if we see considerable overhead technical resistance being surmounted with ease. The company's rising backlog, growing profitability, strong balance sheet, and cheap earnings point to strong potential if the right trading conditions stack up for the shipbuilder. We look forward to continued coverage.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Kroger's Earnings And Why Private Labels Are Especially Appetizing Right Now
data.description:
Thesis: Amid signs of softening consumer spending, I believe value-oriented chains, like Kroger, are well-positioned to maintain steady performance. Kroger’s private labels, in particular, could do well in the months ahead by helping consumers save money (while bolstering KR’s bottom line).

I’ll dig into the details below, but we’re seeing enough signs that consumers are tightening their belts that it’s time to at least consider defensive positions. Kroger’s (NYSE:KR) P/E ratio has risen in recent months, but remains reasonable, especially in a market where high P/E ratios are becoming the norm. Kroger's heavy utilization of private labels, which are typically cheaper than name brands, could prove especially important as consumers shift into value mode.

Kroger's Earnings Recap and Review

Kroger just released its earnings, and overall, the numbers were solid, demonstrating steady growth and success. Zack's consensus estimate pegged EPS at $1.45, but Kroger's adjusted EPS came in slightly higher at $1.49 (Zack's estimate seems to be based on adjusted EPS). Identical sales excluding food were up 3.2%. Revenues, however, fell a bit short of Zach's consensus at $45.1 billion, vs an estimate of $45.38 billion.

E-commerce sales were up 15%, and identical sales grew by 3.2% from a year prior. The operating profit rose from $1.294 billion in Q1 2024 to $1.322 billion in Q1 2025. The declared dividend per common share weighed in at .32 cents, up from .29 cents a year prior.

All in all, KR's quarterly performance strikes me as steady and solid, although certainly not flashy. And that's what I expect from the stock's performance. As for the future, I believe that Kroger's popular store brands, including Private Selection and Simple Truth, make the company an especially attractive investment.

We’re Seeing Clear Signs Consumers Are Tightening Their Belts

I’ve already mentioned in previous articles that evidence is emerging that consumers are tightening their belts. McDonald’s (NYSE:MCD) has seen sales decline after years of steep price increases. Brinker International’s (NYSE:EAT) Chili’s chain is booming with sales driven by their Three-for-Me promo, and Yum!'s (NYSE:YUM) Taco Bell is enjoying success after rolling out a generous customer loyalty program. We’ve seen rising delinquency with credit cards and car loans (for now, primarily in the subprime segment), and credit usage overall is expanding.

New evidence continues to mount. Domino’s and Papa John’s are reporting a decline in delivery orders as customers pick up food instead, likely in an effort to save money. Alter Agents conducted a study and found that many people claim they are now cutting unnecessary spending, reducing eating out, and even canceling or postponing vacations. Meanwhile, loyalty programs, cash back, and the like are becoming more popular.

The sky isn't falling, to be clear. The changes in many cases are subtle, and perceptions could be as important in driving these changes as serious underlying developments. Wages are still rising, unemployment is holding steady, and progress is being made in trade talks.

Still, given all of the above, value-oriented brands may do well, while more expensive businesses may struggle. Upmarket luxury brands may continue to perform well as the luxury market is an entirely different segment, and rising asset prices could be pumping up the holdings of wealthy households. Yet when it comes to the middle and working classes, companies stuck in the middle, like McDonald’s, could be vulnerable.

Kroger is well-positioned in the current market, by my estimation. When people eat out less, they tend to shop at stores more to prepare food at home. When money is tight, people often shift to sales and coupon shopping, which KR can take advantage of. I’ll take a longer look shortly, but with all the above in mind, Kroger’s private-label brands could prove especially popular in the months ahead.

Conditions Could Worsen (But There is No Guarantee)

It’s possible that changing consumer behavior will turn out to be short-term. The trade war that kicked into high gear on “Liberation Day” rattled consumer confidence. If the economy holds strong and employment remains high, consumers may get past trepidations and go back to more normal spending habits.

There are a few concerning points, however. First, college graduates, especially Computer Science engineers, are suffering higher-than-normal unemployment. The Computer Science angle is especially interesting because it could hint at headwinds caused by Artificial Intelligence (but this is speculative, for now).

Meanwhile, the Ludwig Institute for Shared Economic Prosperity (LISEP) recently claimed that the “true unemployment rate” is actually 24.3%. This claim is based on the idea that many people are employed but are struggling to make ends meet. The U-6 unemployment rate, which includes people marginally attached to the workforce, is rather high.

Meanwhile, the labor force participation rate also remains historically low. Fewer people may be unemployed compared to the past, but fewer people are participating in the workforce at all.

I can’t say for certain that the economy is deteriorating. The trends noted above could prove to be a temporary response to the trade war, and businesses are in a “no fire, no hire” mode, which helps explain why college grads are struggling to find jobs. Yet, if the economy remains strong, hiring could pick up again. Still, there are enough concerning trends that make Kroger all the more appealing.

Kroger’s Private Labels Offer Excellent Value

One of my favorite parts about KR, both as a customer and investor, is their private labels. Private labels are designed as substitutes for more expensive name brands. While a name brand company will often have to spend extensive amounts on advertising to lure in customers, private labels typically utilize minimal advertising and instead try to first piggyback off name brands and then lure in customers with more affordable prices. Kroger has previously reported that over 90% of shoppers at their stores have purchased private-label products.

Walmart’s (NYSE:WMT) private labels, in my experience, are a bit iffy, whereas many Private Selection items are good enough that I’d actually take them over name brands even if they are at or nearly at the same price point. (Private Selection premium ice cream > Ben & Jerry’s, especially when price is factored in). Kroger’s Simple Truth brand also offers organics and other upmarket items reminiscent of Whole Foods and Sprouts.

Unsurprisingly, Kroger is one of the most successful private label companies, with generic sales making up more than 22% of revenue. Walmart is slightly ahead, and at the right valuation, would be an excellent choice in the face of inflation, but its current valuation is simply too high for me. Aldi tops the chart, and I’d love the chance to invest in them at a decent evaluation, but it’s a privately held company.

Not only do private labels save customers money, but they actually produce higher profits for grocery stores than name brands. Whereas a name brand is typically marked up 26%, a private label usually offers margins of 35% on average. If inflation picks up, I expect more and more consumers to turn to private labels. Even if inflation remains tempered, consumers are already under enough pressure to look for bargains.

In Kroger’s case, this could turn out to be a major benefit not just right now but going forward. With the right high-quality private label items, the company may be able to build a larger, durable customer base that could stick around even after the economic environment improves. (This is actually how I became a Private Selection/Simple Truth fan. Some tough times more than a decade ago had me tightening my own belt.)

It should also be noted that the high-profit margin enjoyed by private labels will make it more affordable to launch promotions. Kroger also likely won’t need to coordinate with external parties (at least not as extensively) either.

Kroger Maintained Net Income and Revenues During the Great Recession

One of the most appealing things about Kroger is its performance during the Great Recession. While many companies struggled, Kroger soldiered on with revenues gradually rising. The past doesn’t guarantee the future, but I suspect KR will continue to outperform should a recession set in. Share prices could still take a hit and margins could come under pressure, but that might make KR an even more attractive buy.

The chart below shows KR making slow but steady revenue gains even amid the Great Recession. I included Walmart, another value king, to demonstrate that it’s likely not a fluke but more likely part of a general trend.

Kroger Fundamentals and Valuation

Kroger’s P/E ratio is a bit of a concern and is historically rather high, although it has at times been higher in the past. Their GAAP P/E (TTM) is at ~18, compared to 14 at the end of 2022 and below 10 at the end of 2020. Still, the P/E ratio isn’t outside of historical ranges, as the chart below shows.

The P/E ratio is much lower than Walmart’s P/E ratio. Walmart is also a value king, at least as far as store shelf prices are concerned. However, it’s not a value king when looking at P/E ratios, as illustrated below. Some other companies, like Target (NYSE:TGT), have a substantially lower P/E ratio, although I believe Target’s lack of emphasis on groceries could hurt them a bit during a recession.

Gurufocus’s DCF calculator, which I use to compute reverse DCF calculations to forge a baseline to justify current share prices, suggests that Kroger isn’t overvalued. The first calculation uses an EPS measurement (with the discount rate set at 10% per standard, and the terminal growth set at 4%, in line with typically observed global GDP rates). To justify current values, KR needs to maintain EPS growth rates of just 5.78%. When switching to free cash flows (same assumptions), KR needs to maintain a 14.56% growth rate, which, while rather high, is probably doable in the long term.

Seeking Alpha’s quant tools give KR a hold, which in and of the stock itself seems like a fair assessment. However, I support a buy rating owing to the general economic environment and KR’s past performance during hard times. Valuation takes a ding with a C+ rating. Growth is worse with a D+ rating, but since Kroger is limited mostly to the Midwest and South footprint-wise, I believe the company could spur growth with regional expansions.

Kroger owns CityMarket, which operates in flyover states, but the chain has only 34 stores. Kroger also owns Fred Meyer (132 stores), which is centered in Oregon and Washington. The brand operates a number of smaller chains as well, but I believe there’s still room for growth.

Revenue growth, unfortunately, is in the red at -1.94%. While concerning, it’s not enough to turn me away from KR, although I’ll be keeping a close eye on revenue going forward. Forward revenue growth is set at a modest .79%, which, if achieved, would satisfy me for now. The net income margin is only 1.81%. These numbers are a bit lacking, but if consumers do shift into value mode, I believe Kroger can raise the bar.

Take-Away: KR Could Post Steady Long-Term Gains and Provide a Near-Term Haven

Value is king in today’s economy. Even if the turbulence proves to be short-lived and consumer spending and confidence rise further down the road, through at least the next several weeks, I suspect consumers will be careful with their wallets. That makes consumer staples and grocery stores all the more appealing.

Kroger is especially appetizing owing to its private labels, which combine consumer affordability and generally high margins, something I often don’t see these days. Kroger isn’t a flashy stock, and massive upswings are unlikely. Yet, sharp downswings are unlikely as well. While I view KR as a safe option through the near future, I believe it can be a long-haul workhorse stock as well. This was reflected in Kroger's 2025 Q1 earnings report, which showed steady growth overall. Expanding e-commerce sales, by the way, might be able to propel KR beyond my positive expectations in the future.

data.title: FedEx Earnings Preview: Valuation Getting Compelling, But Negative Revisions Continue
data.description:
FedEx (NYSE:FDX), the freight and transport giant, reports their fiscal Q4 ’25 after the closing bell, next Tuesday, June 24th.

The stock is down 20% YTD in ’25 and down -7.77% in the last twelve months after guiding lower for fiscal Q4 ’25 in March ’25, and has now guided the last 3 quarters lower, despite the focus on reducing FedEx’s cost structure by $2 billion.

After becoming positive on FedEx’s prospects after management initiated the cost reduction and FedEx One (consolidation of operations), in early 2023, the earnings release in December ’23 was not a positive and decided to exit the stock. (The problem with FDX as this blog has written many times about the freight giant, which is also true of many industrial companies, is a small slowdown in revenue can cause much bigger changes in EPS thanks to the operating leverage.)

Even with the Freight spinoff, which is due to get completed sometime in fiscal ’26, the stock hasn’t been able to generate any upside.

On Tuesday night, June 24th, 2025, analyst consensus is expecting FedEx to print $5.87 in earnings per share, on $21.8 billion in revenue, and $1.95 billion in operating income, for expected year-over-year (y-o-y) growth of +9%, -1% and +4% respectively.

Eight of the last 11 FedEx quarters have seen negative y-o-y revenue growth. If FedEx meets the revenue estimate Tuesday night, FDX revenue for fiscal ’25 will have been flat, with just 2% expected for fiscal ’26.

Fiscal ’26 guidance today is expecting $19.75 in earnings per share on $87.5 billion in revenue for expected y-o-y growth of +9% and +2% respectively.

The onset of the pandemic in March, 2020, resulted in a huge surge in volume and operating leverage across the FedEx system, only to see this slow in 2022 (and continue to slow) with the rate hikes.

Revenue growth rates (yoy):

  • 4-quarter avg: 0%
  • 12-quarter avg: -1%
  • 20-quarter avg: +6%
  • 40-quarter avg: +7%

Operating income growth (yoy):

  • 4-quarter avg: -2%
  • 12-quarter avg: -2%
  • 20-quarter avg: +19%
  • 40-quarter avg: +9%

Valuation:

FedEx stock is about 10% -15% cheap to perceived intrinsic value (probably not enough of a discount to attract long-term investors given the operating leverage involved at FedEx), at its current price around $223 per share, trading at 12x earnings for expected EPS growth in fiscal ’26 of 9% on 2% revenue growth. The compelling valuation metrics for FDX are the price-to-TTM revenue value of 0.6x, and the price-to-TTM cash-flow metric of 7x. (TTM is trailing twelve month.)

The 6% free-cash-flow yield looks compelling as well.

Trends in EPS and revenue revisions:

Trends in revenue revisions:

Neither of these EPS and revenue revisions portend well for FedEx. The revisions remain negative, and the revenue revisions are more worrisome than the EPS revisions.

The chart:

Summary/conclusion

Looking at XPO Logistics (XPO) and UPS (UPS) stock, both well off their all-time-highs (and UPS is looking far worse than XPO or FedEx,) it’s clear that FedEx’s issues are not all company-related. Increased ground competition from companies like Shein and others, following in Amazon’s (AMZN) footsteps with a proprietary home delivery service, I’ve wondered now these services impact FedEx Ground. (UPS peaked at over $233 per share in early ’22, but is now trading under $100 per share. Haven’t followed the stock fundamentally since the 1990s. No opinion on its investment merits, and never followed XPO either, just looking at charts.)

The negative EPS and revenue revisions are the biggest issue around FedEx right now given the tariff kerfuffle, the sharply higher crude oil price (jet fuel for FedEx), but FedEx can put on a fuel surcharge to protect itself from higher crude prices.

Technically, the upward-sloping trendline for FedEx stock off the Covid March ’20 lows is around the $200 share price (see above chart) or roughly 10% lower than where the stock is trading today.

Clients have no position in the stock other than one longer-term, tax sensitive client that has a FedEx position purchased in March, 2000. The key question for readers though is would this blog commit to a decent weighting in the stock if FDX should trade below $190 – $200 over the next few quarters, and I’m sure that will happen. (Will try and answer that at a later date.)

None of this should be construed as advice or a recommendation but only an opinion. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. The information above may or may not be updated, and if updated may not be done in a timely fashion.

Thanks for reading.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Ryanair: Robust Summer Demand Combined With Pricing Power, Buy Confirmed
data.description:
Our airline coverage has proven solid — following a successful call on International Airlines Group, we’ve recently upgraded Lufthansa Group to a strong buy. We're also pleased with the performance of our Ryanair (NASDAQ:RYAAY, OTCPK:RYAOF) coverage (Fig. 1), which continues to validate our constructive view on the sector. As a reminder, our buy rating on Ryanair was supported by 1) robust traffic trends with A Decade of Growth Ahead, 2) higher earnings estimates that remain ahead of consensus (thanks also to its hedging strategy), and 3) its ultra-low-cost profile underpins our expectation of further market share gains.

With the company having reached our target price and just released its earnings, this is an appropriate moment to refresh our twelve-month outlook.

Fig 1

FY Results and Our Positive View

We note positively Ryanair’s record of carrying 200 million passengers in a single year. In 2025, the company's average fare decreased by 7%, accompanied by a 1% increase in revenue from ancillary sales. Q4 total unit costs were modestly up, with an additional note on the split between fuel and ex-fuel costs. In numbers, sales reached €13.95 billion (by 4% on a yearly basis) with operating expenses rising by 9% to €12.39 billion. Compared to last year, the company's operating profit decreased by 24%. However, upon examining the P&L, Ryanair reported a positive net interest due to a strong cash balance sheet. In March, cash almost reached €4 billion despite €1.6 billion in CAPEX and a complete €1.5 billion share buyback.

Source: Ryanair FY results ppt - Fig 2

Fig 3

Why are we Positive?

  1. Recent geopolitical events in the Middle East are likely to impact the airline sector. There are at least three main downsides: 1) lower traffic growth with impact on direct exposure; however, Ryanair has limited impact on the area, and we also highlight Wizz as having the most significant exposure (among the EU low-cost carriers), 2) lower demand mainly due to negative GDP impact, and 3) oil impact on the airlines P&L (even in this regards, is very well protected compared to the EU industry). In terms of numbers, the company has hedged its oil price at 78% and 84% in 2025 and 2026, respectively (Fig. 5). The aggregate EU airlines have hedged in the 75% area for the current financial year. This provides solid downside protection;

  2. On a positive note, Ryanair will benefit from the Easter shift in Q1 2026 (its calendarized year). During the Q&A analysts call, the company stated that peak summer fares are trending modestly ahead year-over-year, with 30% already booked. Additionally, Ryanair pricing is tracking in the mid-to-high teens, and we see fares even higher due to the high prices from air carriers' flags (and the EU sector consolidation). Aside from easier comparatives, the company restored its OTA network, which might provide volume growth ahead;

  3. Aligned with our supportive equity story in the Lufthansa MRO division, a tight industry supply environment will result in no capacity additions in 2026, and this should aid upward pressure on ticket prices, with Ryanair the first beneficiary;

  4. While some may take issue with the CEO’s remarks, his track record in creating shareholder value remains strong. The company announced a new €750 million share buyback (Fig. 4), which represents 3% of its entire market capitalisation (this came on top of the 7% of shares bought back and already cancelled). This new buyback is likely to run for the next 6-12 months. Top management noted that the buyback could pause in 2026 and 2027 due to bond maturities and CAPEX needs. As a reminder, we note that if the company's shares trade above €21 for 28 consecutive days, the CEO is entitled to 10 million share options to be exercisable at an €11 strike price. That said, the shares will be exercisable in 2028, provided he remains the CEO. We appreciate the precise alignment of interest this creates between the CEO and shareholders.

Fig 4

Fig 5

Earnings Changes and Valuation

Following the release of our full-year 2025 results, we have updated our financial forecasts at the Lab. Ryanair’s guidance came in ahead of our expectations, albeit echoing themes already shared by other travel sector names. Additionally, our team had not anticipated the announcement of a €750 million share buyback, particularly in light of the upcoming bond redemption and macroeconomic uncertainties surrounding travel demand.

That said, we’ve now revised our estimates to reflect the buyback, fuel and FX hedging, as well as updated assumptions for ex-fuel cost inflation and fare trends. Based on the management comment, our fare projections now incorporate quarterly growth assumptions of +15.5%, +2%, –3.5%, and –1% for Q1 through Q4, respectively, resulting in an average increase of roughly 3.75%. This resulted in our sales estimates reaching €15.7 billion. On the cost side, our fuel-adjusted modeling now assumes the ex-fuel cost per passenger will rise approximately 4%, compared to our prior estimate of +2.5%. While we see upside potential to pricing, weaker demand could still lead to fare-driven stimulus. Our EBITDAR reached €3.7 billion, and considering the lower interest income resulting from the buyback, we arrived at a net income of €2.25 billion. Considering the company’s share repurchase and the reduction in shares outstanding from 1.06 billion to 1.028 billion, our EPS increased to 2.18. Overall, these revisions lift our EPS estimates for 2026 by 1.5%.

Given the current fundamentals, we see limited justification for valuing the company at a 20% discount to post-COVID levels; instead, we apply a 12x P/E and 7x EV/EBITDA target as a more balanced reflection of its earnings power. Therefore, we derive a target price of €26.5 per share ($60.8 in ADR).

Risks

Given our in-depth coverage of Ryanair, we highlight several key risks: 1) intensifying competition from low cost operators such as EasyJet and Wizz Air, 2) ongoing aircraft delivery delays that may lead to mismatches in staffing costs (even if might increase the company’s fare price), 3) change in oil price, 4) weakening consumer demand, which particularly affects price-sensitive related to leisure travel, 5) event risks tied to natural disasters, terrorism, and wars (such as the Middle East and Ukraine-Russia conflict), 6) cyberattacks risks, as seen recently with CrowdStrike’s software outage, and 7) airport disruption such as the Heathrow fire. Additionally, in our estimates, we continue to value the company above Wall Street analysts' estimates, and we have removed its 20% discount on a P/E basis. This is primarily due to the company’s dominant market share in the EU and its ability to capture even more market share from weaker players over the coming years.

Conclusion

We continue to rate the company a buy, seeing further upside due to its lower oil sensitivity, a strong management team, and potential for higher sales resulting from industry capacity constraints. The next catalyst will be the Q1 2026 results, which are scheduled to be announced on July 21st.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: XFLT: Valuation Remains Attractive For This 14.6%-Yielding Loan/CLO CEF
data.description:
In this article, we review the XAI Octagon Floating Rate & Alternative Income Trust (XFLT). XFLT allocates primarily to leveraged loans and CLO Equity securities. It is trading at a current yield of 14.6% and a discount of 5.4% as of this writing. XFLT is sub-advised by Octagon Credit, which runs $33bn in credit investments.

Fund Update

As a reminder, the fund's allocation is made up of loans and CLOs. Loans comprise about half of the portfolio with CLOs making up most of the rest. CLO Equity is 37% of the portfolio.

The company's net investment income fell after rising for a few years. The current figure of annualized $0.78 (i.e. $0.39 for the semi-annual period ending 31-Mar-25) works out to a monthly NII of $0.065.

This is slightly overstated as it includes the accrual period when short-term rates were significantly above today's level due to the lags with which loans accrue interest based on short-term rates. This means that the likely distribution coverage today is around 90%.

Interestingly, in the earnings call management did not talk much about short-term rates as the reason for the drop in income and focused more on spread compression in loans, perhaps because it is this factor which caused them to undershoot their own estimates. The chart from Eagle Point Income shows that loan spreads continued to fall into 2025 due to strong risk appetite earlier in the year.

Management said that loan spreads tightened 0.3% over 2024 and a further 0.1% prior to Liberation Day. This dynamic was in large part due to a lack of supply of new loans amid strong demand for CLO exposure and, by extension, loans.

The fund cut its distribution for the second consecutive time to $0.07. Historically, the distribution has tended to follow short-term rates so this was not a surprise.

XFLT is financed with a combination of a credit facility and preferreds. A credit facility is fairly unusual for a CLO fund as it is more vulnerable to a forced deleveraging. However, because only half the fund is allocated to CLOs, XFLT can get away with it. This also allows XFLT to have the lowest cost of leverage among other funds with significant CLO holdings.

XFLT has relatively low fees as well, largely because of a lack of incentive fees.

Its fee structure is only more expensive than that of EIC.

XFLT has more than respectable returns relative to the CLO sector - not far off those of OXLC and ECC which are almost entirely in higher-yielding CLO Equity. Why EIC has been able to deliver a significantly higher return than its higher-yielding sister fund ECC remains a bit of a mystery.

The fund's valuation has cheapened in both absolute terms (right-hand chart) as well as in relative terms vs. the loan sector (left-hand chart). The view that the fund's valuation has cheapened because of pressure on floating-rate asset funds more broadly, is not correct. Its discount is also the second widest in the CLO sector.

XFLT has a term structure with a planned termination date at the end of 2029. This doesn't mean it will definitely terminate as some term CEFs have found a way to wriggle out of it, however it is a distinct possibility. If that happens, its discount will provide a nice tailwind to performance with an additional annualized benefit of 1.9%, something we call the pull-to-NAV yield. This is on the high side relative to the fund's history.

Stance and Takeaways

We continue to hold XFLT in our Income Portfolios. We last topped up our position when its price fell to as low as $5.25. Since then, it has rallied around 10% as markets have recovered. Along with our EIC holding, we prefer funds like XFLT with modest CLO Equity allocations as credit spreads have almost fully retraced their widening, leaving less upside for further CLO Equity outperformance.

We also like XFLT because of its wide discount in the CLO sector which is very attractive in both absolute terms (it is the second cheapest in the sector) as well as relative to its own valuation history. Finally, the fund's management fees are relatively low in the sector due to the lack of incentive fees, allowing the fund to pass on more of its underlying income to investors.

data.title: Building A Better VTI: Why Combining U.S. Large Cap And U.S. Small-Cap Value Makes More Sense
data.description:
Intro

Vanguard Total Stock Market ETF (VTI) is often celebrated as the ultimate set-it-and-forget-it core holding. It captures nearly the entire U.S. equity universe in one ticker—3,567 names across large, mid, and small caps—while charging just 0.03% in expenses and offering world-class liquidity. But simplicity often comes with trade-offs. Buried inside VTI is a long tail of low-conviction small-cap growth names that deliver little to long-term returns. By contrast, a blended sleeve combining a core large-cap fund (VOO), 508 holdings at 0.03% ER, with a focused small-cap value fund (AVUV), 800 holdings at 0.25% ER yields only about 588 unique stocks and a blended fee of roughly 0.085–0.14%. That slight uptick in cost buys you a leaner portfolio, tighter factor exposure, and a stronger historical premium.

Performance

Using long-term historical data from factorlytics.io, we can compare US Large Cap (US LC), US Small Cap (US SC), US Small Cap Growth (US SCG), and US Small Cap Value (US SCV) to evaluate which segments have actually driven returns over time. Keep in mind, factorlytics backtesting these use real ETF returns and OLS-estimated factor loadings—so you’re seeing actual fees, tracking error, and fund-specific tilts, which means results will land a bit below the “perfect” textbook index curves or factor-only synthetic sets. In other words it's a little more realistic based on real ETF or funds you can actually buy to serve as a proxy.

That being said, the model depicts from February 1972 to June 2025, US Large Cap produced a 10.06% CAGR with a 15.41% annualized volatility. Small Cap Growth, despite its narrative appeal, underwhelmed with a 7.72% CAGR and only slightly better volatility. Broad Small Caps (US SC), which include all small stocks regardless of style, earned just 6.84% CAGR and posted the worst max drawdown at over 63%.

In contrast, US Small Cap Value generated a 12.81% CAGR. That’s higher than any other group—and it came with risk, yes, but risk that compensated. The volatility was 17.99%, with a max drawdown of 56.15%. While painful in short stretches, the long-term premium was undeniable. Importantly, SCV’s Sharpe ratio matched SCG’s (0.47), and its Sortino ratio nearly did as well (0.42 vs. 0.43), suggesting the higher volatility was not uncompensated. Meanwhile, the full Small Cap category (US SC) had a dismal 0.11 Sharpe ratio.

This table alone makes a strong argument that VTI’s indiscriminate small cap exposure, outside of US SCV, may be holding investors back. It includes too many of the losers and too little tilt toward the proven winners within the small space.

Compounding Advantage

The real magic of Small Cap Value becomes more evident when you zoom out to portfolio growth. How has it performed when actually blended with Large Cap exposure over time?

The chart shows three strategies: a 100% US Total Market allocation (VTI proxy), a 75/25 US Large Cap/Small Cap Value tilt, and a 50/50 blend. Over the long run, both tilt portfolios significantly outperformed the total market baseline. The 50/50 blend in particular, which leans heavily into Small Cap Value, saw explosive growth post-2000 and again after the 2020 recovery.

That outperformance didn’t come from lucky timing or leverage. It came from better structural exposure. Small Cap Value doesn’t just outperform in isolated cycles. It compounds advantage across time, which makes even modest tilts meaningful over decades.

Rolling Return Cycles

Looking at rolling 5-year CAGRs makes the return dispersion even more apparent. There are clear and sustained periods where a blend of Large Cap and Small Cap Value outpaces the Total Market. This is especially visible during the early 1980s, the recovery period after the Dot-Com Bust, and again in the early 2010s.

What this illustrates is that SCV exposure adds cyclicality to a portfolio that is otherwise dominated by Large Cap tech momentum. That cyclicality doesn’t always pay off immediately, but when it does, it tends to pay off in bursts. These bursts are what lift long-term averages and increase terminal wealth.

Return Distribution and Fat Tail Risk

Another overlooked benefit of tilting toward Small Cap Value is the way it adjusts the return distribution. Monthly return distributions for SCV blends are slightly wider, as expected, but also exhibit higher peaks near modest positive returns. This reflects more frequent positive compounding—exactly what long-term investors want. Meanwhile, Total Market exposure has a narrower distribution and more mass around zero, meaning flat or unproductive periods are more common.

It’s not that SCV avoids drawdowns or volatility. It clearly doesn’t. But its volatility is productive. The tails include extreme positive outcomes, and that’s the kind of tail exposure you want. In contrast, Small Cap Growth tends to load more heavily into negative tail events, especially in bear markets.

Blended Expense Ratio

And while understanding a portfolio’s return distribution and resilience in extreme markets is critical, it’s equally important to remember that fees can quietly erode those gains over time. Below, we break down how blending in a higher-cost small-cap growth fund affects your overall expense ratio—demonstrating why even seemingly small differences matter in the long run.

Portfolio Blended Fee Calculation Blended ER (%)
100% VTI 100% × 0.03% 0.03%
50% VOO + 50% AVUV 0.50 × 0.03% + 0.50 × 0.25% = 0.015% + 0.125% 0.14%
75% VOO + 25% AVUV 0.75 × 0.03% + 0.25 × 0.25% = 0.0225% + 0.0625% 0.085%

Mixing in a higher-cost fund like AVUV can drive your overall expense ratio up by several basis points. In the 50/50 blend, fees surge from virtually zero to 0.14%, which over a 30-year horizon could cut terminal wealth by around 7–10%. Even a modest 75/25 tilt leaves you paying 0.085% instead of 0.03%, trimming roughly 4–5% off long-term growth. When building factor portfolios, it’s worth weighing that fee drag against the extra style exposure you gain. Expense ratios were from Seeking Alpha.

Holdings

Outside of expense ratios, holdings will change. By blending AVUV’s 800 names with VOO’s 508, you end up with just about 588 unique holdings which is roughly one-sixth the size of VTI’s 3,567. In practical terms, you’ve stripped away thousands of small‐cap and marginal “also‐ran” stocks that add very little to total return but can drag on performance through higher bid/ask spreads, lower liquidity, and added noise. What remains is a lean, high-conviction sleeve of true small-cap growth and core large-cap leaders—delivering clearer factor exposure, tighter risk control, and lower trading frictions, without the clutter of the long, underperforming tail. Holdings data is from Seeking Alpha.

Takeaway

VTI remains an elegant, ultra-low-cost way to own the market, but it’s a blunt instrument. Splitting your equity sleeve into US Large Cap and US Small Cap Value lets you keep the broad market’s stability while shedding thousands of marginal small-cap growth stocks. Historically, that tilt has produced higher CAGR, better risk-adjusted returns, and more efficient diversification—all for a modest increase in blended expenses. Over decades, that small adjustment can translate into a significant edge in total wealth.

Personally, instead of holding VTI, I prefer to anchor VOO with a good small cap value fund. I prefer (AVUV) if you've read any of my other articles here, here, and here.

Attribution

All data sourced via factorlytics.io and Seeking Alpha. Additional insights derived from academic literature on factor investing, historical asset class returns, and real-world market structure observations.

data.title: TSLY: Back To Normal, Back To A Sell (Downgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

I've been covering the YieldMax TSLA Option Income Strategy ETF (NYSEARCA:TSLY), more than any other YieldMax ETFs (YM) fund, and boy do they have a lot of them, so it's not for a lack of choices.

Why does TSLY stand out to me?

Well, first, its dividend is absolutely massive.

Secondly, Tesla Motors (TSLA), the asset TSLY sells covered calls against, is very volatile and has produced a significant amount of income via a covered call overlay. YM is one of few firms that do single-stock covered call funds, and so TSLY became one of its standout funds quickly after launch, now over $1B in assets.

I've had a mixed relationship with TSLY, ranging from strong sell to hold ratings (never a full buy), and since I first covered it back in October 2023, its price has ground down over 60% and its return has been less than the S&P 500 including dividends paid, but not reinvested (total return).

Of course, investors did make money, the net between the massive dividends paid, and the price loss was over 24%, but this kind of return pattern doesn't make for the kind of fund that I want to own, or believe it is prudent to advise others to own. There are some exceptions to this rule, like the 0DTE funds that I have come to admire for their unique strategies, despite them suffering from similar return patterns.

However, those funds are largely based on indices, and so they come with less idiosyncratic risk. I don't have to ask the question, "What's going on with Elon Musk?" when I own funds based on the Nasdaq 100, but I do when the fund is based on TSLA.

The Hold Thesis Has Passed

I initially upgraded TSLY to a hold from a strong sell based on the thesis that Elon Musk (TSLA's CEO, for those somehow not in the know) was going to have access to more government contracts and opportunities for Tesla as well as his other private ventures, which TSLA trades in partial sympathy with.

This was due to Musk's appointment as "Efficiency Czar," or the head of the Department of Government Efficiency. That was all speculation at the time, but when it actually happened, and President Trump had that Tesla moment on the White House lawn, the hold thesis was playing out well.

Was that a good thing for TSLA, and therefore TSLY?

Yes, until it wasn't.

Now, President Trump and Musk are not speaking, and in fact, Musk's political presence is harming Tesla since it is in the crosshairs for government contract removal.

The opposite of what I expected when Musk got the gig as head of DOGE is now playing out.

Musk is out of his role officially and is back to tweeting about government rather than helping run it.

However, the damage has been done, and it's clear to investors that Tesla is heavily politicized and may stay that way for at least the rest of this current administration. If electric vehicle tax breaks come back into fashion in politics, or the courts allow states to mandate the end of gas-powered car sales (like in California, where I live), the thesis may be back in action. That seems unlikely given our current political environment.

So if Musk is back to "business as usual" as the Tesla CEO, isn't that a good thing for Tesla, and therefore TSLY?

Time will tell, but what I can say for sure is that damage has been done to the relationship I originally thought would produce value for TSLA shareholders, and keep TSLY producing high income without the level of losses it's sustained.

The title of this article is "back to normal", in reference to Musk leaving DOGE, but I do expect him to remain in the political spotlight for a while. That is likely a new normal for Musk, and frankly has been since his acquisition of Twitter.

This negates the hold thesis I originally had for TSLY, and so I am downgrading the fund back to a sell.

TSLY Overview

Let's get into TSLY itself; it is a single-stock covered call fund, which means that the fund owns a long position in TSLA and sells call options against that position. This caps the upside that the fund is able to receive, but in exchange, it receives a credit for the sale of the option. This credit is what is primarily distributed to shareholders in TSLY's income.

The fund's returns are almost entirely driven by its dividends, and this is very evident in this next chart:

The stock has had to reverse-split before and may do so again if the price gets too low. YieldMax doesn't have a hard rule on when the split happens, as far as I know, but it typically happens when these funds reach below $5/share historically. The only split in its history so far happened back in early last year, denoted by the "S" in the chart below.

TSLY charges a 0.99% p.a. expense ratio and is actively managed.

TSLY Strategy

The fund itself employs a synthetic covered call strategy, which means that it uses options to build its long position and its short position on TSLA. This means that the whole fund is beholden to options risks like early assignment, etc., though it historically has been managed well on the options front by YM.

For those interested in a full breakdown of the options strategy "by the leg," I suggest taking a look at this article I wrote on AMZY, the Amazon version of TSLY, where I called it a "role model for YieldMax ETFs."

TSLY's strategy is to buy and hold T-Bills, essentially cash, and use them to secure its synthetic options positions. This makes their holdings look like this:

Note the options at the bottom. While they do not take up a lot of the fund's NAV, options come with capital efficiency (read as: leverage) built in, so a 3.65% position in the TSLA 340C expiring on 7/18 gives much more exposure than just 3.65%.

For example: buying that call (as of time of writing, 6/19), would cost $1580 as a debit. However, the call gives you a current delta of 40. This value changes as the price of Tesla moves.

Delta, for those who don't "speak Greek," is a measure of how much exposure to the price of the asset a given option (or a whole strategy if you employ net delta). The "quick and dirty" take on delta is approximating how many shares of stock you have exposure to.

40 shares of Tesla stock would cost, using a current price of $322, a total debit of $12,880. Instead of shelling all of that cost out, investors can pay 1/10th of that for the 340C and get a very similar long exposure.

That being said, options aren't "free money" in that way, and are very much unlike owning stock because they can fluctuate in their exposure, potentially reducing or expanding the delta figure, and do not give the same benefits as owning stock (voting, etc.), and most importantly, they expire at some point.

TSLY also sells a put to pair with the call, essentially emulating the profit/loss profile of Tesla stock outright. This is the "synthetic long" strategy and allows for the fund to use options to recreate TSLA exposure with less capital. That means more can be allocated to cash and get the current (relatively high) cash yield on its holdings as well.

The third and final part of the strategy involves selling calls against the synthetic long position, which reduces the net delta, and thus the amount of TSLA upside TSLY captures. However, selling calls provides a premium to the fund, which is then distributed to investors.

Giving up the upside of TSLA for income is just part of the plan, and investors should expect TSLY to trail TSLA overall. This is common among covered call funds and isn't a TSLY exclusive, per se. This strategy is about creating income and not outperformance.

TSLY Dividends

Speaking of income, TSLY pays a variable income, as it is based on the performance of its managers, who actively trade the options held in TSLY.

Dividends have been in decline, but so has the fund's price. The price is grinding down faster than the dividends, especially due to TSLA's losses absorbed by TSLY and only padded by the income received, not hedged by any means.

While the yield has gone up considerably this year, keep in mind that it's also because the YTD chart for TSLY looks like this:

Of course, this is still an "income machine", as it is very unlikely that TSLY will get rid of its dividend or reduce it on purpose. As far as I can tell, YM operates in accordance with its name, and the reduction in dividend is not a factor of them wanting to or intending to cut, but just a product of the reduction in share price. That is why the yield is actually elevated compared to last year, despite the dividend being lower.

One of the considerations for investors with this fund is its tax-efficient income. I've covered the strategy in-depth a lot already, so I won't get too long here. Essentially, an options premium is classified as a return of capital (RoC) which is not taxed at ordinary income rates. Instead, RoC distributions are counted against your cost-basis, which means the taxable event is not the distribution itself, but whenever shares are finally sold. When that happens, investors owe taxes as capital gains, which is typically a lower rate than income.

For more info, and the IRS take on this, see my recent article on QDTE.

Suitability

This fund, and all single-stock covered call funds, are for aggressive investors only who are willing to take on the idiosyncratic risks of a single stock. Moderate and conservative investors are likely unsuited for this fund and should avoid it.

My sell rating is to say that my guidance for investors allocating to this fund is: that a target allocation of 0% is likely most prudent.

We are back to where we were before Musk gained his political power earlier this year, and may be in worse shape if we speculate about the further devolution of Musk's relationship with President Trump. I won't go that far here, but the fact that we could mean that there is likely more politicization risk still in the stock that could negatively affect TSLY.

Conclusion

Ultimately, the YieldMax TSLA Option Income Strategy ETF (NYSEARCA:TSLY) is going back to my sell list, having evaded it and been brought up to hold on the prospects of TSLA having positive political catalysts that could benefit its business, and thus TSLY. However, the opposite is now playing out, and so it may be prudent for investors to go back to my previous advice: avoid this fund.

There are better options out there for folks wanting an aggressive income fund, like QDTE. Moving from single-stock exposure to a broader index may reduce the dividend yield, but may lead to an outsized total return, which typically matters more to me at the end of the day. This is due to the idiosyncratic risks (and rewards) that stocks carry. TSLA at this current moment in time, seems to be carrying a lot of those risks.

Thanks for reading.

data.title: GBTC: Great Hedge Against Inflation
data.description:
Introduction

The price of bitcoin is currently above 100k, more specifically at 104k. It is about 7k above its all-time high, achieved at 111,951 on May 28, 2025. In today's article, we will analyze in detail the Bitcoin asset and its possible path in the short term. With the aim of making a buy/sell or maintenance recommendation on the investment vehicle Grayscale Bitcoin Trust (NYSEARCA:GBTC).

The GBTC is an investment vehicle (ETP) owned by the investment firm Grayscale that offers a direct exposure to the Bitcoin asset, without the investor having to buy, store or guard it. Being a passive trust, it is not subject to the same financial regulations as a Bitcoin ETF when used. The vehicle always keeps a fixed amount of Bitcoin and issues shares that reflect that holding of them. They do not buy and sell Bitcoin on the market, unless the fund's Authorized Participants (PAs) create or redeem what are known as creation units (creation units).

For example, to create new shares (creation units) an Authorized Participant delivers to the trust a multiple of 25,000 shares in Bitcoins (in kind), i.e. approximately 25,000 × 0.00078771 ≈ 19.7 BTC per unit; in exchange, it receives new shares from the trust. To redeem these actions, the Authorized Participant performs the same process but in reverse. As the reader will understand, each new share issued increases the vehicle's reserves by approximately 0.00078771 Bitcoin.

The relationship could be summarized in the following equation:

Where are we?

Currently Bitcoin is in its third bullish cycle after those of the years 2015, 2018 and 2022. With increased institutional participation in daily Bitcoin purchases triggered by the approval of the different ETFs since January 10, 2024. With the entry of this smarter type of capital, this last cycle is lengthening and probably lasts until the end of this year, having a slighter correction, due to the maturation of this market.

Analysis - Macro & On - Chain

In this section, we will analyze Bitcoin from a macroeconomic and onchain point of view, to facilitate that buy/sell or maintenance recommendation.

First we start by looking at the DXY. The reason for this, is that as seen in the following chart, the level of the DXY clearly marks the end of the Bitcoin cycle. As can be seen in the image, in both 2018 and 2022 the DXY levels were around $88, coinciding with the local highs of the respective cycles in Bitcoin. With respect to the current moment we appreciate that the DXY is at about $100 and decreasing, probably going to look for the levels of 85 -90 that coincide with the maximum in the price of Bitcoin in this cycle.

Secondly, we present the Bitcoin vs MAG 7 Ratio metric. This metric compares how many times Bitcoin's cumulative yield exceeds or doesn't exceed the performance of the Magnificent 7. It is clear from the chart that the ratio peaks coincide with the local peaks for each asset cycle. Currently, the metric comes in a clear uptrend where Bitcoin is currently outperforming the MAG 7 by 5 to 1. From my point of view, as long as this indicator is below 10, the current bullish cycle will remain active and the price of Bitcoin will increase from current levels until it reaches that yield multiple.

After these two metrics, we will begin to analyze Bitcoin from the perspective of the activity of your chain. As the first metric in this section we present the Supply in Profit Trend metric. What we analyze in this metric is what the trend of the supply of both LTH and STH is currently in profits. As you can see in the chart, today more than 90% of Bitcoin's circulating supply (20,000,000) is in profits. This has historically been an early sign of future sales of the asset, so we could initially foresee an imminent price contraction. But there's one peculiarity at the moment, which is the third time in less than 6 months that the metric has reached those levels. For this reason, we rule out a short-term correction and predict a rise in the price in search of new highs, since corrections for this reason already existed at these price levels.

In the graph below, we can see the AVIV Momentum Trend Indicator metric. The active value to investor value (AVIV), relates on a daily basis the flow of currencies that change hands daily in the market, divided by the number of currencies that remain in the hands of investors. When the ratio is greater than 1, it means that the transacted flow exceeds the flow maintained by investors. As seen in the chart, when the ratio exceeds value 2, the asset enters a strong distribution phase. This metric does not distinguish between buy and sell within the transactional flow, so a ratio above 1 should not be taken as detrimental. At the moment, the AVIV ratio is at a value of 1.5, which I consider quite healthy to expose me to the asset today.

The next metric analyzed is the STH - Market Value Realized Value Momentum. This metric relates the market value and realized value of STHs (Short Term Holders), which are investors with coins less than 155 days old. The activity of these investors is important since they are the most influential on the price of Bitcoin in the short term. Currently the metric is at healthy levels, with good momentum (green bars at the base of the chart), which indicates a good time to buy.

Finally, we show our star metric that reflects momentum signals in relation to multiple onchain metrics. The blue bars on the chart mean good buying moments based on the momentum that the metric sets. On the other hand, the orange bars indicate otherwise; weak momentum. As you can see in the graph, we are currently at a good point according to the activity of the chain, which consequently gives us a positive signal of momentum, which I translate as a good entry point.

Conclusion

As a conclusion and to finish the article, if I recommend buying the GBTC vehicle that exposes you directly to Bitcoin. I believe that the current moment is a good entry point and that sooner rather than later the asset will seek new historical highs (ATH), which will benefit the vehicle analyzed. If you already bought and have it in your portfolio, my advice is to keep it for these reasons and because it acts fantastically as a hedge against inflation and the loss of purchasing power of traditional currencies.

data.title: FLOT: No Duration, But Still A Dollar Bet
data.description:
The iShares Floating Rate Bond ETF (BATS:FLOT) is a zero-duration fixed income ETF. This means that in effect there are limited credit risks with most of the ratings around A, and there is no duration risk, meaning no real sensitivity to changes in the yield curve. FLOT is the sort of ETF to typically pick up when you're not sure what else to do. However, we are in a quite rare instance of a rather important fall in the value of the dollar and it is related to trading factors concerning the USD reserve currency status. In other words, it is riskier than usual. The FLOT, while not a Treasury exposure, is exposed to US bonds (USD denominated). We do wonder about the impact of geopolitical developments, specifically the situation around Iran, and how that might impact the dollar. We are more inclined to believe that FLOT, which in effect is a yielding (productive) dollar bet, actually benefits from involvement of the US in the Iranian conflict should that come to pass, as it will demonstrate that the US is still concerning itself with the maritime order (Strait of Hormuz risks) and with the general geopolitical world order which supports the dollar. At the very least, even without the interaction between the USD and the geopolitical considerations, the reversal in oil will nominally mean more yield on USD debt as cuts will likely be pushed out more than hoped, which should support the USD. This reduces the risk to FLOT of being USD denominated.

FLOT Breakdown

FLOT is a little more intensively managed as it needs to keep the duration very low. The expense ratio is 0.15%, which is about in line for the more unusual horizons offered by iShares fixed income ETFs that we've seen. The effective duration is virtually zero, meaning that the yield of the ETF is basically variable, and reflects almost a spot interest rate. Moreover, the debt in FLOT is not just Treasury debt, it's of other institutions as well, mostly in banking and substantially in development banks.

Therefore, there is no issue of duration risk which comes from the relative value of a fixed coupon rising or declining depending on the prevailing rate environment after a bond's issuance. Given that there have been significant changes in the position of the yield curve, avoiding such risks can make sense for any capital allocator.

The significant changes in the yield curve in this instance do relate also to effects on the dollar. While USD denominated investors may not notice as much, USD has been performing rather badly, and it is related to the fact that the change in policy of the US, interpreted through the protectionism and economic nationalism of the US, particularly in the rhetoric of the tariffs and less so in the tariffs themselves, might signal conditions that would reduce the utility of the dollar as a reserve currency. The question around the US reserve currency status has been a trading factor and it has been affecting in particular the long maturity bonds of the US, as the US depends heavily on the USD's utility to be able to generate interest in their debt and to refinance.

The tariffs themselves, to the extent that they might be targeted at major flows from China which is a major US creditor, reduce the exchange of dollars to China that need to be invested somehow. Usually it would be in USD debt, but the risk is that might gradually change for alternative deposits in different currencies. But the other question is around the maritime order. The powerful US military has often acted as a guarantor of free movement of trade flows in the seas. With the US combining its trade protectionism with a foreign policy where they are stating they want to do less for allies in terms of hard power, the inclination towards the USD further reduces, as tacitly the use of the USD as a reserve as widely as it has been was a way to provide the financing base for the US in executing its role as a guarantor of freer maritime trade through the US military budget. The relationship is not particularly arcane and is defining of our times, which is to say that the USD's value is mostly from its reserve currency status and the immense value that the USD provides as an efficient medium of value, and little from the fundamentals of the US balance sheet and the typical dynamics one takes when valuing a currency.

Bottom Line

The latest development has been conflict in the Middle East. This poses risks that would mean avoiding duration is a smart idea. We just covered an ETF that was exposed to duration risk, and we predicted the position that the Fed has just taken to not cut rates yet. While the latest CPI readings were moderate, it was given a major helping hand by the lower oil price where the rule of thumb is a $10 decline in oil prices means a -0.4% or so deflationary vector on the CPI. That benefit has now evaporated as questions around Iran's direct oil supply, as well as risks to more major flows that depend on a clear Strait of Hormuz, have lifted the price of oil once again. While the demand environment remains tepid, these supply considerations have been important. Anything that pushes rate cuts out is bad for duration, and the conflict-ridden environment pressures long-term yields as it signals worse international cooperation which is benignly (productively) deflationary.

However, for FLOT, which doesn't concern itself with duration risks, the conflict in Iran provides an opportunity for the new US administration to demonstrate its actual-fact view of foreign military intervention vis-a-vis a national ally in Israel. With the starting position of the US that Iran should surrender, and Trump stating that his patience is wearing thin, and with Israel having established apparent air superiority for the moment, there is the possibility that the US gets involved even if in some limited way to provide its specialised assets against bunkers that support the Iranian nuclear programme. We believe that the interaction between the conflict and the key risk around the Strait of Hormuz means that involvement in this conflict could reinforce the US' commitment to maintaining some of the world order, in particular the maritime world order, which could support the dollar and revert assumptions the market is making about the new US administration. The dollar has climbed a little in the last couple of days back to levels on the 9th of June, possibly in part due to these factors.

However, there is also a general and less politically inclined reason to not be so bearish on the dollar at the moment and that is for the aforementioned reasons that the reversal in oil is going to push out rate cuts, which improves dollar yields and makes it US debt nominally attractive. We'd consider this a baseline benefit, though we think that a positive change in attitudes towards the USD due to their hard power commitments would do more for the dollar thesis. Otherwise, FLOT remains less attractive as the USD risk is still there even if there is no duration risk.

data.title: Intuitive Surgical: Poor Return Prospects Despite Near-Term Upside From Da Vinci 5
data.description:
Intuitive Surgical's (NASDAQ:ISRG) growth continues to accelerate driven by the rollout of the da Vinci 5 system, and increased adoption of the company's SP and Ion solutions. Procedure growth also remains robust, despite headwinds in bariatric surgery and a potential exhaustion of COVID related surgery backlogs. Growth investments are weighing on margins at the moment though. A situation that could be exacerbated by tariffs. This is only a temporary headwind, and Intuitive Surgical's margins should soon return to 30% plus levels.

I previously suggested that second half guidance would be critical to maintaining euphoric investor sentiment toward Intuitive Surgical's stock. I continue to think that Intuitive's valuation will not matter while investor risk appetite persists, and the company continues to outperform expectations. Despite solid growth, a long growth runway and a large competitive advantage, Intuitive Surgical's valuation will make it difficult for the company to generate strong returns longer term though.

Market Conditions

Procedure growth was strong in the quarter, even though there were fewer business days in the quarter. Procedures in the US increased 13% YoY, despite ongoing bariatric surgery headwinds (mid single digit decline). International procedures grew 24%, supported by solid growth in India, Korea and the UK. This is a positive given that strikes are negatively impacting Intuitive's business in South Korea.

Intuitive Surgical is facing headwinds in China though, which could be exacerbated by recent trade tensions. The Chinese government wants to localize production in a number of critical areas, including robotic surgery, which is making life difficult for foreign companies. While this is only impacting a small part of Intuitive's business at the moment, Chinese companies could become a serious threat in other price sensitive international markets.

Competition is also set to increase outside of China in coming years. In particular, Johnson and Johnson and Medtronic are investing heavily in soft tissue robotic systems. J&J completed the acquisition of Verb Surgical in 2020, a joint venture with Google's Verily subsidiary. This initiative eventually led to the OTTAVA robotic surgical system, which is now approaching commercialization. J&J recently completed the first cases with its OTTAVA robotic surgical system and now plans on submitting a De Novo authorization in the US. J&J also acquired Auris Health in 2019, a developer of robotic systems used in bronchoscopic diagnostic and therapeutic procedures. This enables the company to compete with both the da Vinci and Ion systems.

Medtronic offers the Hugo system, although this is not commercially available in the US yet. Hugo met safety and effectiveness endpoints in a recent US urologic trial though, and Medtronic has submitted the system for FDA approval. It has been suggested that Hugo lacks differentiation versus da Vinci, which could impact demand. This could also be a deliberate choice by Medtronic, designed to address the barrier created by user familiarity with Intuitive's system.

Competition probably won't matter in many markets, as Intuitive is already entrenched. It could make winning expansion markets more difficult, or at least ensure that these markets have less favorable economics.

Intuitive Surgical's systems were used in around 2.7 million procedures in 2024, which is something like 1% share of all surgeries globally (over 200 million surgeries each year). Current products and clearances enable Intuitive Surgical to target around 8 million procedures annually, although there are roughly 22 million soft tissue procedures that the company could eventually address. While this is a large opportunity, it isn't actually that large considering Intuitive's current valuation. There is likely to be a large additional growth opportunity in low income countries though. For example, surgery rates in countries with high health expenditures are over 30x higher than in countries with low health expenditures. Intuitive doesn't have the advantage of a large installed base and users that are familiar with its system in many of these countries, which will make capitalizing on this opportunity much more difficult.

Intuitive Surgical Business Updates

Intuitive's installed base exceeded 10,000 systems in Q1. The company placed 367 da Vinci systems in the quarter, including 147 da Vinci 5 systems (down from 174 systems in Q4) and 19 SP systems. The US was an area of strength in terms of system placements, although performance was mixed internationally. Trade-ins increased 130% YoY to 67, which is being driven by da Vinci 5 upgrades.

Intuitive placed 147 da Vinci 5 systems in the first quarter, bringing its installed base to 509 systems. Supply is still being limited until the company is confident it can properly support a broad rollout. Manufacturing capacity is ramping though, and Intuitive is planning a full launch around the middle of the year.

Intuitive is also targeting 510(k) clearance later in the year for features like real-time surgical video review, real-time 3D model review and visual force feedback gauges. Force feedback is designed to help reduce tissue injuries and shorten the learning curve of the system. Early data suggests that force feedback improves the proficiency of less experienced surgeons, leading to improved patient outcomes. Force feedback instruments are in limited supply though and will only be broadly available later in the year.

FDA clearance was recently expanded for the da Vinci Single Port system. It now includes transanal local excision/resection, adding to prior clearance for transabdominal, transoral, and urologic procedures. Intuitive Surgical is also enrolling additional patients in its IDE for nipple-sparing mastectomy.

Intuitive Surgical is focused on getting customers to use Ion for obtaining tissue samples from the lung (transthoracic needle aspiration biopsies), and believes that it is approaching 50% penetration of lung biopsies in the US. While Ion is currently being driven by lung cancer biopsies, its use will be expanded over time, supporting growth of the system longer term.

Tariffs could have a meaningful impact of Intuitive Surgical's business, both in terms of costs and sales. Intuitive manufactured 98% of its robotic systems in the US in 2024. The company also has manufacturing operations in Europe (70% of endoscopes) and Mexico (80% of instruments and accessories).

While there is significant uncertainty surrounding what tariffs will be implemented and when, Intuitive has given guidance that its cost of sales will rise by 1.7% of revenue in 2025. The negative impact will increase through the year though, meaning the rate exiting the year could be meaningfully higher.

Around half of the impact is related to US-China trade. Tariffs on imports into China could have a significant impact on Intuitive Surgical's ability to successfully compete. Intuitive also imports components from China into the US, which could be subject to high tariffs.

Most of Intuitive Surgical's imports from Mexico are certified under the requirements of USMCA. A small minority of imports are expected to incur a 25% tariff though. Other imports into the US are likely to be subject to lower tariffs.

Potential mitigation actions include optimizing production costs and rebalancing production across the company's existing manufacturing footprint. Intuitive Surgical is also considering potential changes to its supply chain. The tariffs that are actually implemented could also end up being significantly lower, creating less of a near-term gross margin headwind.

While tariffs could have a meaningful impact on Intuitive Surgical's business, the company is taking a wait-and-see approach in regard to pricing. Pricing changes are likely once the current situation is better understood though.

Financial Analysis

Intuitive Surgical generated 2.25 billion USD revenue in the first quarter, an increase of 19% YoY. Systems revenue increased 25%, driven by placements and higher system ASP (1.62 million USD, up 17% YoY). Recurring revenue grew 19% in Q1, driven by strong procedure growth.

Intuitive Surgical expects 15-17% procedure growth in 2025, up modestly from the prior quarter. The broader rollout of the da Vinci 5 system should lead to stronger revenue growth in the second half of the year. Analysts are currently only estimate 16% revenue growth in 2025, which appears too conservative.

The decline in gross margin has been driven by rising facilities costs associated with increased manufacturing capacity. Mix shift towards Ion and da Vinci 5 is also a headwind, as these products currently have lower margins. Intuitive plans on opening new manufacturing facilities in Germany and Bulgaria in 2025, and will expand instrument manufacturing capacity in Mexico, which will add to depreciation expenses and further weigh on gross margins. This will be at least partially offset by revenue growth though. Intuitive expects its pro forma gross margin to fall in the 65-66.5% range in 2025, although this doesn't take into account any tariff headwinds.

Pro forma operating expenses increased 12% YoY in Q1 driven by headcount (500 employee increase sequentially), facilities costs and legal fees. Pro forma operating expenses are expected to grow by 10-14% in 2025. Intuitive also expects 650-750 million USD CapEx in 2025, primarily related to facility construction. This likely means that margins will remain under pressure in 2025. Margins should begin to rebound in 2026 though, as growth investments moderate and da Vince 5 gains adoptions.

Intuitive ended the first quarter with 9.1 billion USD cash and investments. This, along with free cash flow, is being used as part of a 4 billion USD share repurchase plan. While this may seem impressive, it modest relative to Intuitive's 180 billion USD market capitalization.

Conclusion

The rollout of new products is supportive of growth but will continue to weigh margins in the near-term. Margins should begin to recover over the next few years though as Intuitive scales its da Vinci 5, SP and Ion user base. Looking beyond this, competition is set to increase. This will likely have minimal impact in Intuitive's more mature markets due to the preference of existing users for the da Vinci system. International markets will be needed to support growth longer term though, and lower priced competitors could be an issue in these markets. As a result, I tend to think that analysts are underestimating Intuitive's near-term prospects, but are more broadly correct about the company's longer term growth potential.

Increased investor confidence in Intuitive Surgical's competitive positioning and the robotic surgery opportunity are supportive of the company's valuation. Intuitive Surgical's business is maturing though, which will eventually lead to multiple compression. I believe the investors are too focused on the quality of Intuitive's business at the moment, and as a result, the company's valuation has risen to a level that will make future returns poor.
